***Syncarpia glomulifera***, commonly known as the **turpentine tree**, or **yanderra**,[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a tree of the family [Myrtaceae](/wiki/Myrtaceae "Myrtaceae") native to [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") and [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") in Australia, which can reach 60 metres (200 feet) in height. It generally grows on heavier soils. The cream flowers appear in spring and are fused into compound flowerheads.

## Taxonomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Syncarpia_glomulifera&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Taxonomy")]

English botanist [James Edward Smith](/wiki/James_Edward_Smith_(botanist) "James Edward Smith (botanist)") first described the turpentine as *Metrosideros glomulifera* in 1797, from a collection in the Sydney district.[[3]](#cite_note-apniold-3) It was given its current binomial name by German botanist [Franz Josef Niedenzu](/wiki/Franz_Josef_Niedenzu "Franz Josef Niedenzu") in 1893.[[4]](#cite_note-apni-4) Common names include turpentine, luster, red turpentine or red luster.[[4]](#cite_note-apni-4) It was formerly known as *Syncarpia laurifolia* Ten.[[5]](#cite_note-EJ10-5) Two subspecies are recognised, the widespread nominate, and subspecies *glabra* which is found from [Bulahdelah](/wiki/Bulahdelah,_New_South_Wales "Bulahdelah, New South Wales") north to [Kempsey](/wiki/Kempsey,_New_South_Wales "Kempsey, New South Wales") and has smooth leaf undersurfaces.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Syncarpia_glomulifera&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Description")]

In a suitable location, the turpentine grows into a large straight-trunked tree up to 45 or even 55 m (150–180 ft) high with a [DBH](/wiki/Diameter_at_breast_height "Diameter at breast height") of up to 1.3 or even 1.5 m (4–5 ft).[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) On poorer soils it grows as a small tree or even adopts a [mallee](/wiki/Mallee_(habit) "Mallee (habit)") habit.[[7]](#cite_note-Ben98-7) The thick brown bark is fibrous, with deep vertical furrows running down the trunk. The leaves are arranged oppositely on the stems, and pairs grow close together so that they resemble a whorled group of four leaves. On 0.7–1.3 cm long [petioles](/wiki/Petiole_(botany) "Petiole (botany)"), the thick leaves are ovate to ellipictal and measure 7–10 cm (3–4 in) long and 2.5–4.5 cm (1–2 in) across with recurved margins. Their upper surface is a dull dark green, and lower surface much paler, either covered by fine hairs in subspecies *glomulifera* or smooth in *glabra*.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) Flowering takes place from August to December, peaking in September.[[7]](#cite_note-Ben98-7) The cream flowers are fused in groups of seven into compound flowerheads.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) This is followed by the development of the compound fruit – 1 to 2 cm (0.39 to 0.79 in) diameter woody capsules which ripen in summer.[[7]](#cite_note-Ben98-7)

## Distribution and habitat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Syncarpia_glomulifera&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Distribution and habitat")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Turpentine_Springwood_Blue_Mountains.jpg/250px-Turpentine_Springwood_Blue_Mountains.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turpentine_Springwood_Blue_Mountains.jpg)

a large tree at [Springwood](/wiki/Springwood,_New_South_Wales "Springwood, New South Wales"), [Blue Mountains National Park](/wiki/Blue_Mountains_National_Park "Blue Mountains National Park")

The turpentine is found in eastern Queensland and New South Wales, from [Atherton](/wiki/Atherton,_Queensland "Atherton, Queensland") in the far north to as far south as [Murramarang National Park](/wiki/Murramarang_National_Park "Murramarang National Park").[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6)[[8]](#cite_note-floransw-8) North of [Gympie](/wiki/Gympie "Gympie"), the distribution is fragmented, with colonies on the [Blackdown](/wiki/Blackdown_Tableland_National_Park "Blackdown Tableland National Park") and [Consuelo Tablelands](/w/index.php?title=Consuelo_Tableland&action=edit&redlink=1 "Consuelo Tableland (page does not exist)"), [Tinaroo](/wiki/Tinaroo,_Queensland "Tinaroo, Queensland") district and [Windsor Tableland](/wiki/Windsor_Tableland "Windsor Tableland").[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) The habitat most commonly associated with the turpentine is transitional forest between rainforest and wet sclerophyll forest.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) Here it grows as an [emergent](/wiki/Rainforest#Emergent_layer "Rainforest") tree,[[8]](#cite_note-floransw-8) and is associated with such trees as flooded gum (*[Eucalyptus grandis](/wiki/Eucalyptus_grandis "Eucalyptus grandis")*), tallowwood (*[E. microcorys](/wiki/Eucalyptus_microcorys "Eucalyptus microcorys")*), grey ironbark (*[E. paniculata](/wiki/Eucalyptus_paniculata "Eucalyptus paniculata")*), white stringybark (*[E. globoidea](/wiki/Eucalyptus_globoidea "Eucalyptus globoidea")*), yellow stringybark (*[E. muelleriana](/wiki/Eucalyptus_muelleriana "Eucalyptus muelleriana")*), white-topped box (*[E. quadrangulata](/wiki/Eucalyptus_quadrangulata "Eucalyptus quadrangulata")*), rough-barked apple (*[Angophora floribunda](/wiki/Angophora_floribunda "Angophora floribunda")*), brush box (*[Lophostemon confertus](/wiki/Lophostemon_confertus "Lophostemon confertus")*) and various rainforest species.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Ben98-7) It also grows with spotted gum (*[Corymbia maculata](/wiki/Corymbia_maculata "Corymbia maculata")*) and blackbutt (*[E. pilularis](/wiki/Eucalyptus_pilularis "Eucalyptus pilularis")*).[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) On poorer soils, it can grow much smaller in dry sclerophyll forest in association with smooth-barked apple (*[Angophora costata](/wiki/Angophora_costata "Angophora costata")*) and yellow bloodwood (*[Corymbia eximia](/wiki/Corymbia_eximia "Corymbia eximia")*).[[7]](#cite_note-Ben98-7)

Adaptable to a wide range of soils, the turpentine is most suited to moderate to high-nutrient soils, such as clay soil over [Wianamatta shale](/wiki/Wianamatta_shale "Wianamatta shale") in the Sydney region.[[7]](#cite_note-Ben98-7) Valleys and flat areas are highly suitable sites.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) In the Sydney region, the species reaches an altitude of 300 m (980 ft), but this extends to 900 m (3,000 ft) above sea level in northern Queensland.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6)

The turpentine is one of the dominant species of the critically endangered [Sydney Turpentine-Ironbark Forest](/wiki/Sydney_Turpentine-Ironbark_Forest "Sydney Turpentine-Ironbark Forest") ecological community.[[7]](#cite_note-Ben98-7)

The largest known turpentine, located near the Williams River Recreation Reserve in the [Barrington Tops National Park](/wiki/Barrington_Tops_National_Park "Barrington Tops National Park"), New South Wales, measures 7.90 m (26 ft) trunk circumference at breast height, 58 m (190 ft) tall, 20 m (66 ft) crown spread width and "points 517" (points calculated using the National Register of Big Trees formula).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Syncarpia_glomulifera&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ecology")]

The flowers are pollinated by native bees and European honeybees, and possibly flies and moths. The [grey-headed flying fox](/wiki/Grey-headed_flying_fox "Grey-headed flying fox") (*Pteropus poliocephalus*) and [little red flying fox](/wiki/Little_red_flying_fox "Little red flying fox") (*P. scapulatus*) also pollinate the flowers. The [rainbow lorikeet](/wiki/Rainbow_lorikeet "Rainbow lorikeet") (*Trichoglossus haematodus*) and [noisy miner](/wiki/Noisy_miner "Noisy miner") (*Manorina melanocephala*) forage for nectar.[[7]](#cite_note-Ben98-7)

The turpentine regenerates after bushfire by resprouting from its [lignotuber](/wiki/Lignotuber "Lignotuber") and [epicormic buds](/wiki/Epicormic_bud "Epicormic bud"). Turpentine trees are thought to live up to 500 years.[[7]](#cite_note-Ben98-7)

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Syncarpia_glomulifera&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Uses")]

Highly durable, turpentine timber is used in heavy-traffic flooring, for poles and wharves. It resists marine invertebrates and termites, and is one of the most difficult timbers to ignite.[[6]](#cite_note-Boland-6) A hardy and adaptable tree, turpentine tolerates heavy soils and frosts, and is suitable for large gardens and parks, where it provides good shade with its dense canopy.[[5]](#cite_note-EJ10-5)
